votes.reason: The Python code accurately implements the logic of the Coq specification, including the recursive digit summation and binary conversion with identical fuel-based termination and base cases.
The Python code accurately implements the decimal digit summation and binary conversion logic defined in the Coq specification, including the recursive structure and base cases.

The Python code is a direct and faithful translation of the Coq code, preserving the logic of summing decimal digits and converting the result to a binary string. The fuel-based recursion in Coq is replicated in Python, and the base cases and recursive steps match exactly.

The Python code faithfully translates the Coq definitions. Both sum_decimal_digits_aux and nat_to_binary_string_pos_aux use the same fuel-based recursion pattern with identical base cases and recursive logic. The main functions sum_decimal_digits, nat_to_binary_string, and solve_impl match exactly. For the given constraint 0 <= N <= 10000, the fuel (which equals n) is always sufficient for the recursion to complete correctly. The spec function correctly checks equality of output with solve_impl(N).

The Python code faithfully translates the Coq definitions. Both sum_decimal_digits_aux and nat_to_binary_string_pos_aux use the same fuel-based recursion pattern with identical base cases and recursive logic. The main functions sum_decimal_digits and nat_to_binary_string handle the same cases (including n=0 returning "0"). For all concrete inputs N in the valid range (0 to 10000), the fuel provided (N itself for digit sum, and the digit sum for binary conversion) is always sufficient to complete the computation, so both implementations will produce identical results.

The Python code faithfully translates the Coq definitions. Both sum_decimal_digits and nat_to_binary_string use the same fuel-based recursion pattern with identical logic. The fuel value (n itself) is sufficient for both digit sum computation and binary conversion within the constraint N <= 10000. The precondition check and spec comparison produce the same concrete results.
Python mirrors Coq’s digit-sum and binary conversion (including fuel/base cases) and uses the same solve_impl; precondition aligns with nat (nonnegative) and upper bound 10000; spec equality matches.
Python mirrors the Coq logic: same fuel-based digit-sum and binary conversion with correct zero handling; solve_impl and spec match. The precondition aligns for natural numbers (N ≤ 10000).

Python mirrors the fuel-based digit-sum and binary conversion logic, handles N=0 consistently, and the precondition matches Coq’s nat domain (nonnegative) with the same bound. For all nonnegative inputs, outputs coincide.
